passage.text:
IELTS Reading Practice
Health & Medicine
10 Reading Passages  ·  500–600 words each  ·  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)

Contents
Passage 1: Why Sleep Is Important for Your Health
Passage 2: Vaccines — Protecting People from Disease
Passage 3: The Importance of a Healthy Diet
Passage 4: Mental Health — Understanding and Overcoming Stigma
Passage 5: Exercise and Its Benefits for the Body
Passage 6: Antibiotics — A Medical Revolution Under Threat
Passage 7: Diabetes — A Growing Global Health Problem
Passage 8: Cancer — Causes, Prevention, and Treatment
Passage 9: The Global Rise of Obesity
Passage 10: Modern Medicine — From Ancient Remedies to Today

Passage 1: Why Sleep Is Important for Your Health
Topic: Sleep & Health  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Sleep is one of the most important things we do every day. Most adults need between seven and nine hours of sleep each night to feel healthy and function well. However, many people in modern society do not get enough sleep. They stay up late, wake up early for work, or have trouble falling asleep. For a long time, people thought sleep was simply a time when the body "switched off" and rested. Scientists now know that this is not true. During sleep, the brain and body are actually very active. They carry out many important tasks that cannot happen when a person is awake.
Paragraph B
There are two main types of sleep. The first is called Non-Rapid Eye Movement sleep, or NREM sleep. This type has several stages, from light sleep to very deep sleep. During deep NREM sleep, the body repairs itself — muscles grow stronger, the immune system is strengthened, and the body's energy is restored. The second type is called Rapid Eye Movement sleep, or REM sleep. During REM sleep, the eyes move quickly under the eyelids. This is when most dreaming happens. REM sleep is also very important for the brain. During this stage, the brain stores new memories and processes emotions. A healthy night of sleep includes several cycles of both NREM and REM sleep.
Paragraph C
Not sleeping enough — even for just a few nights — can have serious effects on health. In the short term, a lack of sleep makes it harder to concentrate, make decisions, and control emotions. People who are very tired often feel irritable and find it difficult to remember things. In the long term, regularly sleeping too little can increase the risk of serious health problems. These include obesity, type 2 diabetes, and heart disease. This is because poor sleep affects the hormones that control hunger, causing people to eat more than they need. Sleep deprivation also weakens the immune system, which makes it easier to catch infections and illnesses.
Paragraph D
Many people suffer from sleep disorders — conditions that make it difficult to sleep well. The most common is insomnia, which is when a person has difficulty falling asleep or staying asleep. Insomnia affects around 10 to 30 percent of adults and can cause tiredness and low mood during the day. Another common disorder is sleep apnoea. This is when a person's breathing stops and starts repeatedly during the night. It often causes loud snoring and makes people feel very tired even after a full night in bed. Both conditions can be treated. Insomnia is often treated with a talking therapy that helps change unhealthy thoughts and habits around sleep. Sleep apnoea is usually treated with a special device that keeps the airway open during the night.

Passage 2: Vaccines — Protecting People from Disease
Topic: Public Health & Immunisation  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Vaccines are one of the most important tools in modern medicine. They protect people from serious diseases by training the immune system to fight infections. When a person receives a vaccine, it introduces a small amount of a weakened or inactive form of a germ, or a tiny piece of it, into the body. The immune system reacts by producing special proteins called antibodies. These antibodies stay in the body for many years. If the person is later exposed to the real disease, the immune system can quickly recognise the germ and fight it before the person becomes seriously ill. Thanks to vaccines, diseases that once killed millions of people every year are now rare or have almost disappeared completely.
Paragraph B
The history of vaccines began over 200 years ago. In 1796, a British doctor named Edward Jenner noticed that dairy workers who caught a mild disease called cowpox did not usually get the much more dangerous disease smallpox. He tested the idea that giving someone cowpox could protect them against smallpox. His experiment was successful, and his discovery led to the development of the first vaccine. Over the following two centuries, scientists developed vaccines against many other diseases, including measles, polio, tetanus, and hepatitis B. One of the greatest achievements of vaccination is the complete eradication of smallpox. In 1980, the World Health Organization officially declared that smallpox no longer existed in the world.
Paragraph C
When many people in a community are vaccinated, it creates a situation called "herd immunity." This means that even people who cannot be vaccinated — for example, newborn babies or people with certain medical conditions — are protected. This is because the disease cannot spread easily when most people are immune. Herd immunity only works when a high enough percentage of the population is vaccinated. For highly infectious diseases like measles, around 95% of the population needs to be vaccinated to achieve herd immunity. If vaccination rates fall below this level, outbreaks can occur, putting the most vulnerable members of society at risk.
Paragraph D
In recent years, some people have become worried about vaccines and have chosen not to vaccinate themselves or their children. This is sometimes called vaccine hesitancy. One reason for this is the spread of false information about vaccines online, which has made some people believe that vaccines are dangerous. However, the scientific evidence clearly shows that vaccines are safe and effective. Serious side effects from vaccines are extremely rare. The risks of catching a serious disease without vaccination are much greater than any risk from the vaccine itself. Health authorities around the world agree that vaccines are one of the safest and most effective ways to prevent disease. Doctors play an important role in helping patients understand the facts and encouraging them to get vaccinated.

Passage 3: The Importance of a Healthy Diet
Topic: Nutrition & Health  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
The food we eat every day has a huge effect on our health. A healthy diet gives the body the energy and nutrients it needs to work well, fight disease, and grow. Nutrients are the substances in food that the body uses to survive and stay healthy. There are several main types of nutrients. Carbohydrates — found in bread, rice, pasta, and fruit — provide energy. Proteins — found in meat, fish, eggs, and beans — help build and repair the body. Fats are necessary for brain function and absorbing certain vitamins. Vitamins and minerals, which are found in fruits and vegetables, are needed in small amounts for many body processes. A diet that includes a good variety of these nutrients is called a balanced diet.
Paragraph B
Eating badly can cause many health problems. One of the biggest issues in many countries today is obesity — the condition of being very overweight. Obesity is usually caused by eating too many foods that are high in sugar, fat, and calories, while not being physically active enough. It increases the risk of serious illnesses such as heart disease, type 2 diabetes, and certain types of cancer. Another common problem is poor nutrition — when people do not get enough of the right vitamins and minerals. For example, not getting enough iron can cause anaemia, which makes a person feel very tired. Not getting enough vitamin D can weaken the bones. Poor nutrition is a major problem in both rich and poor countries, though for different reasons.
Paragraph C
Health experts around the world give similar advice about what people should eat. They recommend eating plenty of vegetables and fruit — ideally five or more portions a day — as these are rich in vitamins, minerals, and fibre. Whole grains, such as brown rice and wholemeal bread, are better than refined grains because they contain more nutrients and keep people feeling full for longer. Lean proteins, such as fish, chicken, and beans, are recommended instead of processed meats like sausages and bacon, which are high in salt and unhealthy fats. Sugary drinks and snacks should be limited because they provide a lot of calories without many nutrients. Drinking enough water — around six to eight glasses a day — is also an important part of a healthy diet.
Paragraph D
In recent years, there has been a lot of interest in specific types of diet, such as the Mediterranean diet and plant-based diets. The Mediterranean diet is based on the eating habits of countries like Greece and Italy. It includes large amounts of vegetables, fruit, whole grains, olive oil, and fish, with small amounts of red meat and dairy products. Many studies have found that people who follow this diet have lower rates of heart disease and live longer. Plant-based diets — which focus on eating mainly or entirely plant foods — have also been shown to have health benefits, including lower rates of obesity, type 2 diabetes, and heart disease. However, people who do not eat any animal products need to be careful to get enough vitamin B12 and iron, which are mainly found in meat and dairy foods.

Passage 4: Mental Health — Understanding and Overcoming Stigma
Topic: Mental Health  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Mental health refers to a person's emotional, psychological, and social wellbeing. It affects how people think, feel, and behave in their daily lives. Good mental health does not mean being happy all the time. It means being able to deal with the normal stresses of life, work productively, and contribute to your community. Mental health problems are very common. According to the World Health Organization, approximately one in four people in the world will experience a mental health condition at some point in their lives. The most common conditions are depression and anxiety disorders. Other conditions include bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, and eating disorders. Mental health problems can affect anyone — children, adults, and elderly people — regardless of their background or social status.
Paragraph B
Despite how common mental health problems are, many people feel ashamed to talk about them or to ask for help. This is because of something called stigma — negative attitudes and beliefs that society holds about people with mental illness. People with mental health conditions are sometimes wrongly seen as dangerous, weak, or unable to make decisions. These beliefs are harmful and untrue. Stigma can stop people from seeking the help they need, which can make their condition worse. It can also cause discrimination in the workplace or in personal relationships. Many countries and organisations are working to reduce stigma through public education campaigns, encouraging people to talk openly about mental health and sharing stories of people who have recovered from mental illness.
Paragraph C
Fortunately, mental health conditions are treatable. There are many effective options available. Talking therapies — such as Cognitive Behavioural Therapy, often called CBT — help people identify and change negative patterns of thinking and behaviour. Studies show that CBT is one of the most effective treatments for both depression and anxiety. Medication, such as antidepressants, can also be helpful, especially for moderate to severe depression. Many people benefit most from a combination of talking therapy and medication. Lifestyle changes can also play an important role in improving mental health. Regular physical exercise has been shown to reduce symptoms of depression and anxiety. Getting enough sleep, eating well, and having strong social connections are also important for good mental health.
Paragraph D
Mental health services vary greatly between countries. In high-income countries, most people can access mental health support through their national health service or private healthcare. However, even in wealthy countries, many people do not receive treatment because of long waiting times or because they do not know where to get help. In low- and middle-income countries, there is often a severe shortage of mental health professionals. The WHO estimates that in some low-income countries, there is fewer than one mental health worker for every 100,000 people. This means millions of people with serious mental health conditions receive no care at all. Improving access to mental health care, especially in poorer countries, is one of the biggest challenges in global health today.

Passage 5: Exercise and Its Benefits for the Body
Topic: Physical Activity & Health  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Regular physical exercise is one of the best things a person can do for their health. Doctors and health organisations around the world recommend that adults should do at least 150 minutes of moderate exercise each week. Moderate exercise means activities that make you breathe a little harder and your heart beat a little faster than normal — such as brisk walking, cycling, swimming, or dancing. People can also do 75 minutes of more intense exercise, such as running or playing sports, which produces similar health benefits in less time. Children and teenagers need even more physical activity — at least 60 minutes per day. Despite these recommendations, physical inactivity has become a global health problem, with around one in four adults worldwide not getting enough exercise.
Paragraph B
Exercise has many important benefits for the physical body. For the heart and blood vessels, regular aerobic exercise — such as running, swimming, or cycling — strengthens the heart muscle, lowers blood pressure, and reduces levels of harmful fats in the blood. This greatly reduces the risk of heart disease, which is the number one cause of death worldwide. Exercise also helps to control body weight by burning calories and increasing the speed at which the body uses energy, even at rest. Strengthening exercises — such as lifting weights or doing push-ups — build and maintain muscle mass, which is particularly important as people get older, since muscle naturally decreases with age. Strong bones are another benefit: weight-bearing exercise such as walking and running helps maintain bone density and can reduce the risk of osteoporosis.
Paragraph C
Exercise is not only good for the body — it also has powerful effects on mental health. When a person exercises, the brain releases chemicals called endorphins. These chemicals reduce feelings of pain and create a feeling of happiness and wellbeing. This is sometimes called the "runner's high" — a feeling of joy that some people experience after a long run or other intense exercise. Many studies have found that regular exercise is as effective as medication for reducing mild to moderate depression. Exercise also reduces stress and anxiety, helps people sleep better at night, and improves concentration and mental sharpness. Older adults who exercise regularly are less likely to develop memory problems and are at lower risk of conditions like Alzheimer's disease.
Paragraph D
Despite the well-known benefits of exercise, many people find it difficult to be physically active on a regular basis. The most common reasons include lack of time, tiredness after work, not having access to sports facilities, and not enjoying exercise. Health experts suggest several strategies to help people become more active. Finding an activity that you enjoy is one of the most important steps, as you are more likely to continue doing something you find fun. Exercising with a friend or in a group can also help by making it more social and enjoyable. Even small changes to daily routines can make a difference — taking the stairs instead of the lift, walking or cycling to work, or getting off public transport one stop early and walking the rest of the way. The key message from health professionals is that any amount of exercise is better than none.

Passage 6: Antibiotics — A Medical Revolution Under Threat
Topic: Medicine & Disease  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Before the invention of antibiotics, even a small cut or a minor infection could be fatal. Diseases such as tuberculosis, pneumonia, and blood poisoning killed millions of people every year. This changed dramatically in 1928, when Scottish scientist Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin — the world's first antibiotic. Fleming noticed that a type of mould had accidentally grown on one of his laboratory dishes and was killing the bacteria around it. The active ingredient was later identified as penicillin. By the 1940s, penicillin was being produced in large quantities and used to treat soldiers during the Second World War. It saved countless lives. Since then, scientists have developed many different types of antibiotics, which work by killing bacteria or stopping them from growing.
Paragraph B
Antibiotics have been one of the most important advances in the history of medicine. They have made it possible to successfully treat many infections that were once deadly, such as pneumonia, meningitis, and typhoid fever. They are also essential before and after many types of surgery, as they prevent infections that could otherwise be fatal. Without antibiotics, many modern medical procedures — including cancer treatment, organ transplants, and joint replacement surgery — would be far too risky to perform. It is estimated that antibiotics save millions of lives every year. The discovery of antibiotics is considered one of the most significant events in the history of medicine, alongside the development of vaccines.
Paragraph C
However, there is now a growing and serious problem known as antibiotic resistance. This happens when bacteria gradually change over time and develop the ability to survive being treated with antibiotics. When bacteria become resistant, the antibiotics no longer work, making infections very difficult or even impossible to treat. Antibiotic resistance develops when antibiotics are used too often or used incorrectly — for example, when people stop taking a course of antibiotics early because they feel better, or when antibiotics are prescribed for illnesses caused by viruses, such as colds and flu, which antibiotics cannot treat. Farming practices also play a role: in many countries, antibiotics are given to farm animals to help them grow faster, which contributes to the spread of resistant bacteria.
Paragraph D
The World Health Organization has described antibiotic resistance as one of the biggest threats to global health. Every year, around 700,000 people die worldwide from infections caused by resistant bacteria. If no action is taken, this number could rise to 10 million per year by 2050. Governments, health organisations, and scientists are working on several solutions. These include educating doctors and patients about the correct use of antibiotics, restricting the use of antibiotics in farm animals, and investing in research to develop new types of antibiotics. People can help by only taking antibiotics when prescribed by a doctor, always completing the full course, and never sharing antibiotics with others. Preventing infections through vaccination and good hygiene is also very important.

Passage 7: Diabetes — A Growing Global Health Problem
Topic: Chronic Disease  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Diabetes is one of the most common chronic diseases in the world. A chronic disease is a long-term health condition that usually cannot be cured but can be managed. Diabetes affects the way the body manages sugar in the blood. When we eat, the body breaks food down into a type of sugar called glucose, which is used for energy. A hormone called insulin, produced by the pancreas, helps glucose move from the blood into the body's cells. In people with diabetes, this process does not work properly. Either the body does not produce enough insulin, or the body's cells do not respond to insulin correctly. As a result, glucose builds up in the blood instead of being used for energy, which can cause serious damage to many parts of the body over time.
Paragraph B
There are two main types of diabetes. Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune condition, which means the immune system attacks the cells in the pancreas that produce insulin. People with type 1 diabetes produce little or no insulin and must take insulin injections or use an insulin pump every day to survive. Type 1 diabetes is usually diagnosed in children or young adults. The exact cause is not fully understood, but it is not related to diet or lifestyle. Type 2 diabetes is the most common form — it accounts for around 90% of all diabetes cases. In type 2 diabetes, the body still produces insulin but the cells become resistant to it. Type 2 diabetes is strongly linked to being overweight, having an unhealthy diet, and not being physically active. It is most commonly diagnosed in adults, though it is becoming more common in young people.
Paragraph C
If diabetes is not properly managed, it can lead to very serious health problems. Over time, high blood sugar can damage the blood vessels and nerves throughout the body. This can affect many different organs. Damage to the blood vessels in the eyes can cause vision problems and even blindness. Damage to the kidneys can lead to kidney failure. Poor blood flow to the feet can cause wounds to heal very slowly, and in serious cases, toes or feet may need to be amputated. People with diabetes also have a significantly higher risk of heart disease and stroke. Regular check-ups, monitoring blood sugar levels, taking medication, eating a healthy diet, and exercising regularly are all essential for preventing these complications.
Paragraph D
The number of people with diabetes is growing rapidly around the world. In 1980, about 108 million adults had diabetes. By 2022, this number had risen to over 800 million. This dramatic increase is mainly due to the global rise in obesity, unhealthy diets, and sedentary lifestyles. The good news is that type 2 diabetes is largely preventable. Studies have shown that making lifestyle changes — such as losing weight, eating more vegetables and less sugar, and exercising regularly — can significantly reduce the risk of developing type 2 diabetes. For people who already have type 2 diabetes, these same changes can help to manage blood sugar levels and may even reverse the condition in some cases. Raising public awareness about the risks of diabetes and encouraging healthier lifestyles is a key goal for health organisations around the world.

Passage 8: Cancer — Causes, Prevention, and Treatment
Topic: Disease & Medicine  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Cancer is a disease in which cells in the body grow and divide in an uncontrolled way. Normally, cells grow, divide, and die in an organised process. Cancer occurs when something goes wrong with this process and some cells begin to grow and divide without stopping. These cells can form a lump called a tumour. Some tumours are benign, which means they do not spread to other parts of the body. Malignant tumours, however, can spread to other organs and tissues — a process called metastasis. Cancer is not a single disease. There are more than 100 different types of cancer, named after the part of the body where they start — for example, lung cancer, breast cancer, skin cancer, and bowel cancer. Together, cancers are one of the leading causes of death worldwide.
Paragraph B
Many factors can increase a person's risk of developing cancer. Some risk factors cannot be changed — for example, age (cancer is more common in older people), genetics (some people inherit genes that increase their cancer risk), and gender (some cancers are more common in men, others in women). However, many risk factors are linked to lifestyle choices and can be modified. Smoking is the single biggest preventable cause of cancer — it is responsible for about 15% of all cancer deaths worldwide. Heavy alcohol consumption also increases the risk of several cancers. Being overweight, eating a diet low in fruit and vegetables, and not being physically active are also significant risk factors. Prolonged exposure to the sun's ultraviolet rays greatly increases the risk of skin cancer.
Paragraph C
Early detection of cancer greatly improves the chances of successful treatment. This is why many countries have cancer screening programmes, which test people for signs of cancer before they have any symptoms. For example, women are encouraged to have regular breast screening (mammograms) to detect breast cancer early. Bowel cancer screening tests can find problems before they develop into cancer. Cervical smear tests can detect abnormal cells in the cervix that could become cancer if not treated. When cancer is detected at an early stage, it is usually smaller and has not spread, making it much easier to treat. People are also advised to learn the warning signs of cancer — such as an unusual lump, unexplained weight loss, or changes in a mole — and to see a doctor promptly if they notice anything unusual.
Paragraph D
Cancer treatment has improved enormously in recent decades, and many types of cancer that were once fatal can now be treated successfully. The main treatments are surgery, radiotherapy, chemotherapy, and immunotherapy. Surgery involves removing the tumour from the body. Radiotherapy uses high-energy rays to destroy cancer cells. Chemotherapy uses powerful drugs to kill cancer cells or stop them from growing. Immunotherapy is a newer type of treatment that helps the body's own immune system recognise and attack cancer cells. Many patients receive a combination of these treatments. Although cancer treatment can be effective, it often has significant side effects, such as tiredness, nausea, and hair loss. Research into new and better treatments continues, with the goal of finding more effective ways to treat cancer with fewer side effects.

Passage 9: The Global Rise of Obesity
Topic: Public Health  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Obesity is a condition in which a person has too much body fat, to the level where it begins to harm their health. It is usually measured using a number called the Body Mass Index, or BMI, which compares a person's weight to their height. A BMI of 25 to 30 is considered overweight, and a BMI above 30 is considered obese. Over the past few decades, obesity has grown rapidly around the world. In 1975, fewer than one in ten adults in the world were obese. By 2022, this figure had risen to nearly one in eight. Today, more than one billion people worldwide are obese. This includes a growing number of children and teenagers. The World Health Organization has described obesity as a global epidemic.
Paragraph B
Obesity has many causes. The most basic cause is a long-term imbalance between the amount of energy a person takes in through food and the amount of energy they use through physical activity. When people eat more calories than they burn, the extra energy is stored as fat. However, the reasons why this happens are often complex. The modern food environment plays a big role — processed foods and sugary drinks are often cheap, easy to find, and heavily advertised, making them difficult to avoid. Many people live sedentary lives, spending long hours sitting at work or at school and commuting by car or public transport rather than walking or cycling. Genetic factors can also affect how likely a person is to gain weight, as some people's bodies store fat more easily than others.
Paragraph C
Being obese significantly increases the risk of many serious health conditions. Heart disease is one of the biggest risks, as obesity causes high blood pressure and high levels of fat in the blood, which can block the blood vessels. Type 2 diabetes is also closely linked to obesity — around 80 to 85% of people with type 2 diabetes are overweight or obese. Obesity also increases the risk of certain types of cancer, joint problems, breathing difficulties, and liver disease. In addition to these physical health effects, obese people are also more likely to experience mental health problems such as depression, low self-esteem, and social isolation, often as a result of discrimination and negative attitudes from others.
Paragraph D
Tackling obesity requires action at both an individual and a societal level. For individuals, making healthy food choices and being more physically active are essential steps. Losing even a small amount of weight — just 5 to 10% of body weight — can significantly improve health and reduce the risk of complications. For people who are severely obese, medical treatments such as weight-loss medication or surgery may be recommended when lifestyle changes alone have not been successful. At a societal level, governments can introduce policies to reduce the availability and attractiveness of unhealthy foods, such as taxes on sugary drinks, regulations on advertising junk food to children, and improving access to green spaces and sports facilities. Creating healthier schools and workplaces is also important.

Passage 10: Modern Medicine — From Ancient Remedies to Today
Topic: History of Medicine  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Medicine has been practised for thousands of years. In ancient times, people had very limited understanding of how the body worked or what caused disease. Many believed that illness was caused by spirits or gods, and treatments were often based on religion and magic rather than science. However, even in ancient times, people developed some effective medical knowledge through careful observation. Ancient Egyptians created some of the earliest written medical records, describing treatments for wounds, broken bones, and various illnesses. In ancient China and India, complex medical systems — including herbal medicines and acupuncture — were developed. The ancient Greeks made important contributions, particularly through the physician Hippocrates, who is often called the "father of medicine." He taught that disease had natural causes and that doctors should observe patients carefully and make rational decisions about treatment.
Paragraph B
For many centuries, medical knowledge changed very slowly. During the Middle Ages in Europe, most medical ideas were based on the teachings of ancient Greek and Roman doctors, and little new knowledge was added. The most significant progress began during the Scientific Revolution in the 16th and 17th centuries, when scientists began to use experiments and observation to understand the natural world. In 1628, the English doctor William Harvey proved that blood circulates around the body — a major discovery that changed the understanding of how the heart and blood vessels work. The invention of the microscope allowed scientists to discover germs — tiny organisms that could cause disease. This led to the "germ theory of disease," developed by scientists including Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch in the 19th century, which completely transformed medicine.
Paragraph C
The 20th century saw enormous advances in medicine that dramatically increased life expectancy in many parts of the world. The discovery of penicillin in 1928 by Alexander Fleming led to the development of antibiotics, which could cure many previously deadly bacterial infections. The development of vaccines eliminated or greatly reduced many serious diseases, including smallpox, polio, and measles. Advances in surgery — including the development of anaesthetics, which allow patients to have operations without feeling pain, and antiseptic techniques that prevent infection — made complex surgical procedures much safer. Medical imaging technology, such as X-rays, CT scans, and MRI scans, allowed doctors to look inside the body without performing surgery. By the end of the 20th century, average life expectancy in many countries had risen from around 50 years to over 75 years.
Paragraph D
Today, medicine continues to advance at a rapid pace. New technologies such as genetic testing allow doctors to understand a patient's individual risk of developing certain diseases and to personalise their treatment. Robot-assisted surgery enables surgeons to perform complex operations with greater precision and smaller cuts, leading to faster recovery times for patients. Research into new medicines, including targeted cancer therapies that attack only cancer cells without harming healthy tissue, is producing more effective and less harmful treatments. Telemedicine — the use of video calls and apps to consult with a doctor remotely — has made healthcare more accessible, particularly for people in rural areas or those who have difficulty travelling. Despite all this progress, significant challenges remain, including the unequal access to healthcare between rich and poor countries, and the growing threat of antibiotic-resistant bacteria.
